---
title: "Modeling Degree Progress as Fine-Grained Mastery Learning: Applications of Generative AI in Adaptive Tutoring and Credit Mobility"
author: 
  - name: Zach Pardos
    affiliation: School of Education
date: '2026-04-06'
image: zach.jpg
categories: "Spring 2026"
css: /seminarentries/speakerpages.css
---

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
Abstract: This presentation explores the prospect of conceptualizing college degree progress as a continuous framework of fine-grained mastery learning for transfers and others taking non-normative paths through higher education. First, I will discuss empirical studies demonstrating the efficacy of Large Language Models (LLMs) in generating psychometrically sound questions and pedagogically effective hints within Intelligent Tutoring Systems, building upon the open-source Open Adaptive tutor project being piloted at Cal. Second, I will extend this micro-level learning framework to macro-level institutional progress by examining stakeholder-informed AI methodologies for course articulation. Specifically, I will detail a supervised alignment method implemented within the SUNY system that mitigates institutional catalog biases, achieving a 5.5-fold improvement in accuracy and significantly expanding credit mobility. By leveraging representational learning techniques that can map learning objectives across contexts, the presentation will end with a questioning of how more flexible pathways can be constructed while retaining the value of a full-package University experience. 
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
![{{< meta author >}}]({{< meta image >}})
:::

:::::
